food_subgroup: Chinese braised and simmered mixed dish with meat, tofu, vegetables, and glass noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Beijing-style family hotpot assortment. FOODON_03315595 (hot pot food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (composite meat-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Beijing-style hotpot assortment code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept exists for this specific Beijing-style mixed hotpot assortment.

main_ingredients: glass noodles / mung bean vermicelli (粉条)

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared family-style serving of approximately 600 g, intended for 2–3 persons, comprising broth, crispy pork, meatballs, tofu, napa cabbage, glass noodles, dried shrimp, and tomato.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: pork belly (cooked), pork meatballs, firm tofu, glass noodles (mung bean), napa cabbage, tomato, dried shrimp, and broth; weighted by approximate ingredient proportions in a typical Beijing-style mixed hotpot assortment.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Beijing-style family hotpot assortment. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted calculation using USDA FoodData Central entries for each major ingredient. Approximate ingredient weight proportions used: crispy pork belly 15%, pork meatballs 20%, firm tofu 15%, glass noodles (cooked) 15%, napa cabbage 15%, tomato 10%, dried shrimp 3%, broth/water 7%. Values represent a plausible per-100 g composite and carry medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous components: glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli) GI ~26–39 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); firm tofu GI ~15; pork and shrimp protein components GI ~0; tomato GI ~15; napa cabbage GI ~10. Weighted composite estimate yields GI ~42 for the mixed dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Beijing-style family hotpot assortment. GI estimated at 42 (low category) based on weighted contribution of major glycemic components: glass noodles (mung bean) GI ~33 (University of Sydney GI Database), firm tofu GI ~15, tomato GI ~15, napa cabbage GI ~10, and protein/fat components (pork, shrimp) contributing GI ~0. Glycemic load per 600 g serving calculated as (42 × 56.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 23.7, rounded to 22.6 after moisture adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~2280 mg per 600 g serving) from broth, dried shrimp, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g and request reduced-sodium broth, as a full 600 g serving may provide approximately 2280 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive adults.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~22–24 per full serving) driven primarily by glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli) and tofu carbohydrates, partially offset by high protein and fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume a moderate portion (200–300 g) of this dish as the low GI (~42) and high protein content support glycaemic control, but should monitor total carbohydrate intake from glass noodles and pair with non-starchy vegetables to limit postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g) from pork belly and meatballs, combined with high sodium from broth and dried shrimp
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to a 200–250 g portion, preferring the tofu, cabbage, and glass noodle components over the crispy pork belly to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, and should request low-sodium broth where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijing-Style Family Hotpot Assortment (京味合家欢, Jīng wèi hé jiā huān) is a Chinese communal dish originating from Beijing culinary tradition, served as a shared family-style platter. The dish features a rich, savory broth as its base, into which multiple ingredients are simmered together: crispy deep-fried pork belly (鲜香酥肉) that softens in the broth while retaining a layered texture, hand-formed pork meatballs (手打肉丸) with a springy, dense bite, dried shrimp (海米) that impart an umami depth to the broth, tender napa cabbage (娃娃菜) that absorbs the savory liquid, smooth glass noodles made from mung bean starch (粉条) that provide a silky, slippery texture, firm northern-style tofu (北豆腐) with a slightly chewy exterior, and fresh tomato (西红柿) that contributes a gentle acidity and sweetness to balance the richness. The dish is a hallmark of Beijing home cooking and is associated with family gatherings and festive occasions. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density (~112 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein (~7.8 g per 100 g) from pork, meatballs, shrimp, and tofu, moderate fat (~5.2 g per 100 g) with some saturated fat from pork, and a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~42) driven by the mung bean glass noodles and tofu. Sodium content is elevated (~380 mg per 100 g) due to the seasoned broth and dried shrimp, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean shellfish allergies. It is dairy-free. Clinically, the low GI and high protein content make moderate portions suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, while the sodium load warrants caution for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients.

Is this dish suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Beijing-style family hotpot assortment as served by the restaurant. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted calculation using USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy) entries for each major ingredient. GI is estimated from analogous components using the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in USDA FoodData Central and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central; no direct approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the component foods of this dish. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (~42, low category) based on weighted contributions of glass noodles (mung bean), tofu, tomato, cabbage, and protein/fat components; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 4. Glycemic load per 600 g serving is approximately 22.6–23.7 g. 5. Sodium content is elevated due to seasoned broth and dried shrimp; exact sodium will vary by restaurant preparation. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as served at See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 (Singapore); pork is a primary ingredient. 7. Allergens include crustaceans (dried shrimp) and soy (tofu); gluten may be present in meatball binders. 8. Multilingual names follow Singaporean Chinese dish conventions (English, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per Project Veritas v2 rules. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry medium confidence. 10. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries for this dish.
